basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: al-Nabaʾ — “the Announcement” — opens on the murmur of men questioning one another: “About what are they questioning?” The great Announcement (al-nabaʾ al-ʿaẓīm) over which they divide is the Resurrection. The sūra answers not with dispute but with the unfolding of the signs of creation — the earth as a couch, the mountains as pegs, sleep as rest, night as a garment, day for life, the seven heavens, the blazing lamp, the water that descends from the clouds. Then comes the Day of Decision: the trumpet, the gates of heaven flung open, the mountains become a mirage. Qushayrī opens the sūra with an admirable page on adornment: the adornment of the worshippers is the cloak of their obedience, that of the gnostics the robe of their knowledge, that of the lovers the crown of their sainthood — and the adornment of sinners is to wash their faces with the streaming of their tears. Onto the verse “We have numbered all things in a Book” he grafts a meditation on the days of separation of the tried lovers, which too are counted; and in the denier's wish “would that I were dust!” he reads the lament of Iblīs, regretting that he was not drawn, like Adam, from the earth.

versets.trad: About what are they questioning one another?
versets.commentaire: ʿamma yatasāʾalūn (عَمَّ يَتَساءَلون): about what are they questioning one another? Qushayrī: they divide “by the harshness of their denial of the Resurrection, by the confusion it casts them into, and by the multitude of their questions to the Prophet about it.” The sūra opens on the rustle of an anxious rumour: they argue, they question, they doubt under their breath. The question itself betrays the trouble of a heart that senses a truth it does not wish to receive.

versets.trad: About the momentous Announcement
versets.commentaire: al-nabaʾ al-ʿaẓīm (النَّبَأ العَظيم): the great Announcement, the immense news. Qushayrī: “the great news” — the Resurrection. The word nabaʾ does not speak of mere information but of a weighty announcement, heavy with consequence. The greatness of the news matches what it reveals: that life does not end at death, that all will be raised and judged. It is the announcement that changes the meaning of all existence.

versets.trad: about which they disagree.
versets.commentaire: mukhtalifūn (مُخْتَلِفون): in disagreement, divided. Before the great Announcement, men split apart: some believe, others deny, others waver. The division is not in the truth but in the hearts that receive it. The same news illumines one and disturbs another: it is not the Announcement that changes, but the disposition of the one who hears it.

versets.trad: And yet, soon they will know!
versets.commentaire: kallā sa-yaʿlamūn (كَلّا سَيَعْلَمون): no indeed! soon they will know. The kallā cuts off the debate: the dispute will end, not by arguments but by evidence. What one refuses to believe, one will end by knowing — but then it will be too late for that knowledge to avail. The threat is also a promise: truth does not depend on our assent, it will impose itself of its own accord.

versets.trad: Truly, soon they will know!
versets.commentaire: A redoubling of the previous warning. The repetition adds not information but intensity: this is no idle threat. As in sūra al-Takāthur, this hammering imitates the ineluctable. Qushayrī hints that this knowing will come in two times: that of death, where the veil begins to lift, and that of the Resurrection, where it falls altogether. They will know — first on crossing the threshold, then before the unveiled Real.

versets.trad: Did We not lay out the earth as a couch
versets.commentaire: mihād (مِهاد): a couch, a cradle, an outspread bed. Qushayrī: “We made it docile for them, that they might dwell upon it.” Here is the first of the signs the sūra sets against doubt: before arguing about the Resurrection, God shows creation. The earth is no hostile soil but a prepared bed, as one sets up a cradle for the awaited child. Providence is inscribed in the very form of the world.

versets.trad: and the mountains as pegs?
versets.commentaire: awtād (أَوْتاد): pegs, stakes. Qushayrī: “pegs for the earth, that it may not sway with them.” The image is that of the tent: the mountains pin down the ground as the pegs stretch the canvas and keep it from blowing away. The world is set up like a dwelling for the guest, and each element plays its part of balance. Nothing is left to chance in this prepared house.

versets.trad: We created you in pairs,
versets.commentaire: azwājan (أَزْواجًا): in pairs, in couples. Qushayrī: “male and female, fair and unfair, and the rest.” Creation proceeds by polarities: not uniformity, but the fertile play of opposites and complements. The pair is the sign that life is born of encounter, not of solitude — and that the diversity willed by God is itself a blessing, not a flaw to be corrected.

versets.trad: We made your sleep a repose,
versets.commentaire: subāt (سُبات): rest, cessation. Qushayrī: “a rest for you, that you may cease the movements that wearied you in the day.” Sleep is a daily mercy: each night God suspends the toil of the day. It is also a little death, and a sign: He who withdraws your consciousness each evening and restores it each morning can well restore your life after death. The daily waking prefigures the resurrection.

versets.trad: We made the night a covering,
versets.commentaire: libās (لِباس): a garment, a covering. Qushayrī: “its darkness covers every thing, that you may find rest in it.” The night clothes the world like a cloak, veils the toils and uglinesses of the day, wraps the sleeper. A delicate metaphor: night is not the absence of light but the presence of a protective veil. What seems privation — darkness — is in truth an embrace that soothes.

versets.trad: We made the day a time for living,
versets.commentaire: maʿāsh (مَعاش): the time of livelihood, of active life. Qushayrī: “the time of your living.” To the night-garment answers the day-work: God has woven the alternation so that man rests and toils in turn. The rhythm of day and night is a wisdom: neither exhausting wakefulness nor endless sleep, but the balanced breathing of a life in which effort and rest relieve one another.

mots.def: maʿāsh: means of living, time of activity.

versets.trad: We built above you seven firm [heavens],
versets.commentaire: sabʿan shidādan (سَبْعًا شِدادًا): seven firm ones — the seven heavens. Qushayrī: “the seven heavens.” Above the earth-couch rises the seven-storeyed vault, firm and well built. The image completes the dwelling: an outspread floor, a solid roof. Creation is an architecture, not a chaos: all is built (banaynā) with measure and firmness, as one builds a house meant to last.

mots.racine: س ب ع / ش د د

versets.trad: and We set a blazing lamp.
versets.commentaire: sirāj wahhāj (سِراج وَهّاج): a blazing lamp — the sun. Qushayrī: “it is the sun; We made it a burning, blazing lamp.” The sun hung from the vault is the lamp of the great dwelling. Source of light and heat, it makes possible the life of the day (the maʿāsh of verse 11). Like a lamp the master of the house lights for his guests, the sun bears witness to an intention: creation is illumined to be inhabited.

versets.trad: We sent down from the rain-clouds an abundant water,
versets.commentaire: al-muʿṣirāt (المُعْصِرات): the clouds pressed by the winds, glosses Qushayrī, “the winds that press the clouds”; māʾ thajjāj: a water that pours in torrents. The image is that of the winepress: the winds press the clouds as one presses the grape, and the water streams. This water that falls from heaven to give life to the earth is, too, a sign: He who sends down the water that resurrects the dead soil will resurrect the dead.

mots.def: muʿṣirāt: clouds laden with rain / pressing winds.

mots.racine: م و ه / ث ج ج

versets.trad: to bring forth with it grain and plants
versets.commentaire: ḥabban wa-nabātan (حَبًّا وَنَباتًا): grain and vegetation. Qushayrī: “grain like wheat and barley.” The water sent down is not an end but a means: it brings forth life from the earth. The grain, dead in appearance, germinates and feeds: a daily proof that life springs from what seemed lifeless. The whole sūra weaves this lesson: the resurrection is but God's agriculture brought to its term.

mots.gram: lām of purpose + subjunctive verb + pronoun

versets.trad: and luxuriant gardens.
versets.commentaire: jannāt alfāf (جَنّات أَلْفاف): dense gardens, glosses Qushayrī: “orchards whose trees interlace one with another.” The water that brought forth the grain (v. 15) also produces gardens so dense their branches entangle. And Qushayrī concludes the argument: “If you have seen all this, how can you not know that I am able to remake creation and raise the Resurrection?” The teeming of life is the signature of the Living.

versets.trad: Truly, the Day of Decision will come at the appointed term.
versets.commentaire: yawm al-faṣl… mīqātan (يَوْم الفَصْل ميقاتًا): the Day of Decision is an appointed term. After the signs of creation, here is the conclusion: this Day has its assigned term, as everything in creation has its measure (cf. s. 77, v. 22). Mīqāt — the agreed moment — says that nothing is left to chance: the Resurrection is no possibility but a meeting already inscribed. He who has disposed all things with measure has also fixed the hour of the reckoning.

versets.ar: يَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ فَتَأْتُونَ أَفْوَاجًا
versets.tr: yawma yunfakhu fī l-ṣūri fa-taʾtūna afwājan
versets.trad: That Day the trumpet will be sounded and you will come in throngs.
versets.commentaire: afwājan (أَفْواجًا): in throngs, in companies. Qushayrī: “that Day, you will come in groups and cohorts.” The blast in the trumpet (al-ṣūr) summons the resurrected, who flow in waves toward the place of gathering. A striking image of the whole of humanity set in motion by a single breath. The distinctions of the world — ranks, fortunes, nations — dissolve in this throng summoned at the same hour.

mots.sens: the day the trumpet is sounded

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ف و ج
mots.sens: and you will come in throngs

versets.trad: The heaven will be opened, and become many gates;
versets.commentaire: fa-kānat abwāban (فَكانَتْ أَبْوابًا): and it became gates. Qushayrī: “it split and was rent.” The solid seven-storeyed heaven (v. 12) opens on all sides, turning into so many gates. What was a closed vault becomes a passage. An image of unveiling: the barrier between the visible and the invisible falls, and what was hidden behind the heaven appears. The long-shut gates all open together.

versets.trad: the mountains will be set in motion and become a mirage.
versets.commentaire: fa-kānat sarāban (فَكانَتْ سَرابًا): and they became a mirage. The mountain-pegs (v. 7), the very symbols of stability, set off in motion and dissolve into a mirage — that illusory water which flees the thirsty traveller. A vertiginous reversal: what seemed most solid proves as insubstantial as an illusion. On the Day, all of man's false assurances are revealed for what they were: mirages.

mots.sens: and the mountains are set in motion

versets.trad: Truly, Gehenna lies in wait,
versets.commentaire: mirṣād (مِرْصاد): a place of watch, an ambush — or an obligatory passage. Qushayrī gives both senses: “a place of passage” and “a watch-post lying in wait for its people.” Gehenna is not only a distant place: it lies in wait, on everyone's path, watching who advances. A fearsome image: the evil one has fled does not sleep, it waits at the turning. But this watch is such only for the one who has destined himself to it by his deeds.

versets.trad: the place of return for the rebellious.
versets.commentaire: li-l-ṭāghīn maʾāban (لِلطّاغين مَآبًا): for the rebellious, a place of return. Qushayrī: “a place to which they return.” al-ṭāghūn: those who have overstepped all measure (same root as the ṭughyān of Pharaoh, s. 79). One returns to what one has chosen: the one who made excess his inner dwelling returns to it at the end. The word maʾāb — place of return — echoes, in dark mirror, the maʾāb toward the Lord of verse 39: to each, the return he has prepared.

mots.def: ṭāghī: one who oversteps all measure.
maʾāb: place one returns to, final abode.

versets.trad: There they will tarry for ages,
versets.commentaire: aḥqāb (أَحْقاب): ages, durations beyond number. Qushayrī: “epochs (duhūr), in the sense of forever.” The word evokes cycles of time succeeding one another endlessly. The gravity of excess is measured by the duration of its consequences: what was committed in little time is paid in ages. The image is meant not to terrify alone, but to give its true weight to the present choice.

versets.ar: لَّا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا بَرْدًا وَلَا شَرَابًا
versets.tr: lā yadhūqūna fīhā bardan wa-lā sharāban
versets.trad: tasting there neither coolness nor drink,
versets.commentaire: lā bardan wa-lā sharāban (لا بَرْدًا وَلا شَرابًا): neither coolness nor drink. The twofold deprivation is the exact opposite of the earth's gifts recalled earlier: the shade of the mountains, the sweet water (s. 77, v. 27). What refreshed and quenched in creation is withdrawn. Deprivation is no arbitrary addition: it is the absence of what one refused to acknowledge as a gift. The one who did not give thanks for the sweet water finds himself without water.

versets.trad: only a scalding water and a foul discharge.
versets.commentaire: ḥamīm wa-ghassāq (حَميم وَغَسّاق): a scalding water and a freezing, fetid liquid. A cruel irony: the only “drink” is what burns or what repels. To the two extremes — the furnace and the putrid cold — is lacking the just mean, that temperance which made the felicity of the Garden (s. 76, v. 13: neither burning sun nor biting cold). The chastisement is the absence of balance: the soul that has lost measure finds itself tossed between the extreme opposites.

versets.trad: a fitting recompense.
versets.commentaire: jazāʾan wifāqan (جَزاءً وِفاقًا): a fitting recompense. Qushayrī: “they are recompensed in accord with their deeds” — or “in accord with what the decree foreordained and the judgement established.” The word wifāq speaks of exact correspondence: the penalty fits the fault, neither more nor less. There is no arbitrariness in the chastisement: it is the faithful reflection of the act, as felicity is the reflection of love. One reaps precisely what one has sown.

versets.trad: They did not expect to render any account,
versets.commentaire: lā yarjūna ḥisāban (لا يَرْجونَ حِسابًا): they expected no account. Qushayrī: “they did not believe, hoping for reward and fearing punishment.” Here is the root of excess: to live as if one would never answer for anything. The one who expects no reckoning believes himself without limits — and this illusion of impunity is the source of all overflows. To acknowledge that one will render account is already to begin to set oneself right.

versets.trad: and persisted in treating Our signs as lies.
versets.commentaire: kadhdhabū bi-āyātinā kidhdhāban (كَذَّبوا بِآياتِنا كِذّابًا): they denied Our signs with utter denial. The absolute object kidhdhāban underlines the intensity: not a passing doubt but a systematic denial. The signs (āyāt) unfolded throughout the sūra — the earth, the heavens, the rain, the gardens — were received as lies. Denial is not ignorance but a deliberate refusal to read reality as sign. One shuts one's eyes to what nonetheless cries out the Creator's presence.

mots.def: kadhdhaba: to treat as a lie.

versets.trad: Yet We have numbered all things in a Book.
versets.commentaire: aḥṣaynāhu kitāban (أَحْصَيْناهُ كِتابًا): We have numbered it, recorded it in writing. Nothing escapes the divine register. But Qushayrī opens here an unexpected pearl: if God counts the faults of the disobedient and the deeds of the obedient, He also counts “the days of separation of the forsaken (mahjūrīn) and the days of trial of the tested (mumtaḥanīn) — and in this there is a consolation for them.” The same Book that troubles the guilty comforts the tried lover: God forgets neither our failings nor our sorrows. Every tear shed in separation is, too, counted.

versets.trad: So taste! For We shall increase you in nothing but torment.
versets.commentaire: fa-dhūqū falan nazīdakum illā ʿadhāban (فَذوقوا فَلَنْ نَزيدَكُمْ إلّا عَذابًا): taste, We shall increase you only in chastisement. A verse tradition holds to be the most terrible of the Quran. But Qushayrī turns it into a vast luminous symmetry: “O you, blessed of the Garden, rejoice: We shall increase you only in reward. O obedient ones, rejoice: We shall increase you only in favour upon favour.” To each, the increase of what he has chosen: the law of the Day is that all grows in the direction in which the heart has turned. The same verb — “to increase” — parts two eternities.

versets.trad: Truly, for those who fear God there will be a triumph:
versets.commentaire: mafāz (مَفاز): the place of success, the triumph, salvation. A luminous reversal after the torment of the rebellious: for the God-fearing, the mafāz. The word speaks both of escape from danger and of access to bliss — to escape the Fire and reach the Garden are one and the same movement. Qushayrī: “what We have promised them is guaranteed to them.” To the fitting recompense of the guilty (v. 26) answers the guaranteed triumph of the God-fearing.

versets.trad: orchards and vineyards,
versets.commentaire: ḥadāʾiq wa-aʿnāb (حَدائِق وَأَعْناب): walled gardens and vines. The triumph (v. 31) unfolds into concrete images: orchards encircled by walls, trellises laden with grapes. To the luxuriant gardens of earthly creation (v. 16) answer the gardens of Paradise, their fulfilment. What the earth offered as a sign, the Garden offers in fullness: the beauty glimpsed here below was but a promise of the one that abides.

versets.trad: maidens of equal youth,
versets.commentaire: kawāʿib atrāb (كَواعِب أَتْراب): companions of equal youth, of the same age. The image speaks of harmony: not disparity but accord — beings matched, in perfect concord. Beyond the letter, the Garden is the place where all is in accord, where the discordance that wounds the soul here below no longer reigns. Equal youth figures the freshness that does not wither and the peace of a company without dissonance.

mots.def: tirb: of the same age, matched.

versets.trad: and a brimming cup.
versets.commentaire: kaʾsan dihāqan (كَأْسًا دِهاقًا): a cup full to the brim, overflowing. An image of fullness without lack: the cup never empties. In the spiritual language of the neighbouring sūras (s. 76, v. 21; s. 77, v. 43), the cup is the drink of intimacy with God. That it overflows says that felicity has no restrictive measure: to the one who has opened to God, the gift is poured without counting, ever full, never running dry.

versets.ar: لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا وَلَا كِذَّابًا
versets.tr: lā yasmaʿūna fīhā laghwan wa-lā kidhdhāban
versets.trad: There they will hear neither idle talk nor lies:
versets.commentaire: lā laghwan wa-lā kidhdhāban (لا لَغْوًا وَلا كِذّابًا): neither idle chatter nor lie. Qushayrī gives the inner reading: “their ears are guarded from hearing what is other than Him (al-aghyār), and their sights protected from gazing at forms and traces.” The Garden is first a purified silence: no more vain words, no more lies that defiled this world. And more deeply, the ear of the heart hears nothing but Him: the supreme felicity is to listen to none but the Beloved.

versets.trad: a recompense from your Lord, a well-reckoned gift
versets.commentaire: jazāʾan… ʿaṭāʾan ḥisāban (جَزاءً عَطاءً حِسابًا): a recompense, a sufficient gift. The word ʿaṭāʾ — free gift — quietly corrects jazāʾ — earned recompense: what seems wage is in reality bounty. And ḥisāban: a “well-reckoned” gift, that is, amply sufficient, in exact measure of the need and beyond. The felicity of the God-fearing is no due wrested but a present from the Lord: grace always surpasses merit.

mots.racine: ج ز ي / ر ب ب

versets.ar: رَّبِّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا الرَّحْمَٰنِ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْهُ خِطَابًا
versets.tr: rabbi l-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-mā baynahumā l-raḥmāni lā yamlikūna minhu khiṭāban
versets.trad: from the Lord of the heavens and the earth and all that is between them, the Most Merciful, with whom they will have no power to argue.
versets.commentaire: lā yamlikūna minhu khiṭāban (لا يَمْلِكونَ مِنْهُ خِطابًا): they have no power to address Him a word. Qushayrī exclaims: “how could the fashioned creature, poor and wretched, have the power to address Him a word, or to breathe a breath without Him? No — He is God, the One, the All-Compelling.” Before the Lord of the heavens, the earth, and what lies between, none has initiative: one speaks only if He permits. And yet this absolute Master is named al-Raḥmān, the All-Merciful: omnipotence does not crush, it reveals itself as mercy.

mots.sens: Lord of the heavens and the earth

mots.racine: م ل ك / خ ط ب
mots.sens: they cannot address Him a word

versets.ar: يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الرُّوحُ وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ صَفًّا لَّا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَقَالَ صَوَابًا
versets.tr: yawma yaqūmu l-rūḥu wa-l-malāʾikatu ṣaffan lā yatakallamūna illā man adhina lahu l-raḥmānu wa-qāla ṣawāban
versets.trad: On the Day when the Spirit and the angels stand in ranks, they will not speak, save the one whom the Most Merciful permits and who speaks rightly.
versets.commentaire: yaqūmu l-rūḥu wa-l-malāʾikatu ṣaffan (يَقومُ الرّوحُ وَالمَلائِكَةُ صَفًّا): the Spirit and the angels stand in ranks. Qushayrī opens here a profound distinction: “reverent majesty (hayba) appears, on that Day, upon all the people of the gathering. But the elect, the people of presence (aṣḥāb al-ḥuḍūr), are from of old in the contemplation of Majesty (mashhad al-ʿizz), clad in this reverent awe, with neither rest nor respite: the veil of majesty envelops them and its realities overpower them.” For the common, hayba is the dread of a day; for the intimate, it is the permanent state of the heart held under the Gaze. And none speaks, save by permission and to speak rightly (ṣawāban): on the threshold of the Presence, true speech is a gift, not an initiative.

mots.racine: ق و م / ر و ح
mots.def: rūḥ: the Spirit (Gabriel, or a supreme angel).

mots.racine: م ل ك / ص ف ف

mots.sens: save the one the Most Merciful permits

versets.ar: ذَٰلِكَ الْيَوْمُ الْحَقُّ فَمَن شَاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِ مَآبًا
versets.tr: dhālika l-yawmu l-ḥaqqu fa-man shāʾa ttakhadha ilā rabbihi maʾāban
versets.trad: That will be the Day of Truth. Let whoever wills take the way of return toward his Lord.
versets.commentaire: al-yawm al-ḥaqq (اليَوْم الحَقّ): the Day of the Real, of Truth. Qushayrī, in a dense formula: “they are in the contemplation of the Real (al-Ḥaqq), the judgement upon them is the Real, He judges them by the Real, and they are drawn (majdhūbūn) by the Real toward the Real.” On the Day of Truth, all is brought back to the One: the veil of illusion falls and only Reality subsists. And the door stays open: fa-man shāʾa — “let whoever wills take a return (maʾāb) toward his Lord.” To the maʾāb of the rebellious, which is Gehenna (v. 22), answers this maʾāb toward the Lord: two returns, the same word, opposite senses according to the heart that chooses.

mots.sens: that is the Day of Truth

mots.racine: ر ب ب / أ و ب

versets.ar: إِنَّا أَنذَرْنَاكُمْ عَذَابًا قَرِيبًا يَوْمَ يَنظُرُ الْمَرْءُ مَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاهُ وَيَقُولُ الْكَافِرُ يَا لَيْتَنِي كُنتُ تُرَابًا
versets.tr: innā andharnākum ʿadhāban qarīban yawma yanẓuru l-marʾu mā qaddamat yadāhu wa-yaqūlu l-kāfiru yā laytanī kuntu turāban
versets.trad: Truly, We have warned you of a near chastisement, on the Day when man will behold what his hands have sent ahead and the denier will cry: “Woe to me! Would that I were dust!”
versets.commentaire: A closing verse, where all converges. ʿadhāban qarīban (عَذابًا قَريبًا): a near chastisement. Qushayrī: “far in the eyes of the heedless, but in truth near.” The Day when each will behold what his hands have sent ahead: man before the pitiless mirror of his deeds. And the cry of the denier: “would that I were dust!” Qushayrī (the author) recognizes in it the lament of Iblīs: “Would that I had been created like Adam, from earth, instead of saying: I am better than him, for I am of fire!” Yesterday's proud refusal becomes today's regret. And Qushayrī's last lesson on the whole sūra: “they passed in the abasement of choice (ikhtiyār) and toil, and were raised in the regret of vain wishing; had they been content with the decree (taqdīr), they would have been delivered from wishing.” The secret of salvation holds in this surrender: not to will otherwise, but to consent to what is.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / ق ر ب
ن ظ ر / م ر أ
ق د م / ي د ي
mots.sens: what his hands have sent ahead

mots.def: layta: would that; turāb: dust, earth.
